VERAXA Biotech Establishes in vitro Proof-of-Concept for Novel BiTAC-ADC Technology Platform and Launches Partnering Discussions at BIO International Convention 2026 BiTAC-ADC in vitro data, along with initial data from the most advanced BiTAC-TCE program recently presented at the American Association for Cancer Research (AACR) Annual Meeting, underpin the Company’s two viable and clearly differentiated product platform technologies June 18, 2026 07:00 ET | Source: VERAXA Biotech GmbH Follow VERAXA Biotech GmbH Zurich Share ZURICH, SWITZERLAND – June 18, 2026 -- VERAXA Biotech AG (NASDAQ: VRXA; “VERAXA”), an emerging leader in designing novel cancer therapies, today announced that the Company has generated new in vitro proof-of-concept data validating its novel BiTAC-ADC technology platform and its potential to enable more precise and targeted cancer therapies. VERAXA will attend the BIO International Convention in San Diego, Calif., June 22-25, 2026, to discuss partnering opportunities based on its novel BiTAC-ADC and BiTAC-TCE platforms. The BiTAC TM therapeutic strategy is designed to unlock a new level of precision in cancer treatment by using two complementary precursors and enabling a tumor-restricted activation of the desired therapeutic effect. Applied to the development of antibody drug conjugates (ADCs), the technology delivers a systemically inactive prodrug and a cell-impermeable proactivator through two separate antibodies, each addressing a defined tumor-associated antigen. While the single BiTAC-ADC precursors are therefore not toxic by design, the selective internalization of both components into the same cancer cells leads to dose-dependent cytotoxicity. In in vitro studies, BiTAC-ADCs have now been shown to discriminate between breast cancer and healthy cells and have demonstrated efficient and dose-dependent killing of 3D tumor cell spheroids. “Following our successful listing on NASDAQ earlier this month, the BIO International Convention presents a great opportunity and is the ideal timing to provide more details on how our BiTAC-ADC platform can be applied to the potential benefit of cancer patients,” said Heinz Schwer, Ph.D., MBA, Chief Business Officer of VERAXA. “Current ADC technologies have transformed cancer treatment but continue to face challenges associated with off-tumor toxicity caused by payload exposure in healthy tissues. BiTAC-ADCs are specifically designed to address this issue. By doing so, the platform could allow the use of very potent payloads in more settings than previously possible, with the ambition of improving both safety and targeted efficacy of next-generation ADC therapeutics.” “While still early in development, the data now available for our BiTAC-ADC platform indicate that our concept of selectively delivering and activating a toxic payload in tumor cells is working,” said Christoph Erkel, Ph.D., Vice President Research & Development at VERAXA. “Following the presentation of our BiTAC-TCE strategy at the recent AACR Annual Meeting in April, these novel datasets on the BiTAC-ADC platform underpin that the company has two viable and clearly differentiated product platform technologies, which can be applied in a range of solid tumor indications by us and our potential future partners.” About VERAXA Biotech AG (NASDAQ: VRXA) At VERAXA, we are building a premier engine for the discovery and development of next-generation antibody-based therapeutics, including bispecific T cell engagers, bispecific ADCs and other innovative formats. Powered by a suite of transformative technologies and guided by rigorous quality-by-design principles, we are rapidly advancing our pipeline of ADCs and proprietary BiTAC formats into clinical development and beyond. VERAXA was founded on scientific breakthroughs made at the European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL), a world-renowned institution known for pioneering life science research and cutting-edge technology. For regular updates about VERAXA Biotech, visit https://investors.veraxa.com/ or follow us on LinkedIn , X (formerly known as Twitter) and Bluesky . articles.title: Asimov Launches Chrysalis Transposase, a Wholly-Owned Genome Integration Tool for Biotherapeutics Production
articles.text:
Accessibility: Skip TopNav Asimov Announces Strategic Partnership with the Rosalind & Morris Goodman Cancer Institute at McGill University to Advance Next-Generation... Asimov Announces Strategic Partnership with the Rosalind & Morris Goodman Cancer Institute at McGill University to Advance Next-Generation Biologics Asimov will provide cell line development and process development services to support promising biotherapeutic molecules identified through McGill’s oncology research programs June 18, 2026 07:00 ET | Source: Asimov Follow Asimov Share BOSTON, June  18, 2026  (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Asimov, the company building an AI-native synthetic biology platform to advance therapeutic development, today announced a strategic partnership with the Rosalind & Morris Goodman Cancer Institute (GCI) at McGill University. Through the collaboration, Asimov will provide cell line development and process development services to researchers at the GCI and McGill, supporting next-generation biologic candidates as they progress through IND-enabling studies and toward clinical development. “The GCI has a long track record of fundamental advances in cancer biology. As a strategic platform partner to the GCI, Asimov is excited to support the PIs and broader team as they continue pushing the boundaries of cancer research,” said Alec Nielsen, co-founder and CEO at Asimov. “As we expand our platform into earlier stages of drug discovery, including AI-powered multispecifics design, our goal is to make sure leading research institutions like the GCI have the best tools behind their science at every step.” “The GCI's mission is to translate fundamental cancer discovery into therapies that improve outcomes for patients, and that means giving our scientists access to technologies that match the molecular complexity of today's most promising therapeutic candidates,” said John Stagg, PhD, Director of the Rosalind and Morris Goodman Cancer Institute at McGill University. “Asimov's platform gives our researchers the ability to go from a promising design to a manufacturable molecule and a high-titer cell line, and we look forward to working with the Asimov team to shorten the distance between a discovery in our labs and a therapy that helps patients.” Over the past fifteen years, advances in clinical oncology have enabled the development of checkpoint inhibitors and other advanced biologics that have changed how clinicians treat cancer. The field is now moving rapidly into complex, multispecific architectures that engage multiple immune and tumor targets at once, opening up mechanisms that a single binder cannot achieve. Bridging the gap between discovery and manufacturability is still a major challenge for many researchers, as the bioproduction systems most academic labs have access to today were not designed for the level of molecular complexity seen in next-gen therapeutic candidates. As a result, many promising biologics are produced in relatively low-titer cell lines, creating a bottleneck on the path from a compelling discovery to a candidate ready for IND-enabling studies. The GCI sits at the forefront of efforts to translate fundamental cancer research into clinical outcomes, with research programs that integrate AI, computational biology, and advanced cancer modeling to uncover the mechanisms driving cancer and identify new therapeutic targets. Through this partnership, GCI and McGill researchers will gain access to Asimov's CHO Edge System, an AI-powered cell line development platform that routinely achieves titers of 8 to 12 g/L across biologic modalities, from standard IgGs to more complex architectures including multispecifics and fusion proteins. CHO Edge combines Asimov's proprietary GS knock-out CHO host, hyperactive transposase, and genetic parts library with Kernel, Asimov's computer-aided genetic design software, which uses AI models trained on Asimov's experimental database to recommend molecule-specific vector designs. About Asimov Asimov’s mission is to advance humanity’s ability to design living systems, enabling biotechnologies with outsized societal benefit. The company is developing a synthetic biology platform—from cells to software—to design and manufacture next-generation therapeutics, including biologics, cell and gene therapies, and RNA through a combination of products, services, and collaborations. Founded by bioengineers from MIT and Boston University and headquartered in Boston, the company has raised over $200 million from top institutional investors including Andreessen Horowitz, CPP Investments, Horizons Ventures, and Fidelity Management & Research Company. For more information, visit www.asimov.com . About the Rosalind & Morris Goodman Cancer Institute at McGill University A part of McGill University’s Faculty of Medicine and Health Sciences, the GCI brings together world-leading scientists and clinicians in a collaborative environment tackling the most important problems in cancer research through innovative thinking and a multidisciplinary approach. Supported by state-of-the-art infrastructure, the GCI is a recognized world-class research Institute contributing to innovative developments in the treatment and ultimately the cure for cancer. For more information, visit www.goodmancancer.ca . Contact media@asimov.com Tags multispecific antibody antibody biotech biotechnology cell line development synthetic biology Related Links Asimov GlobeNewswire Release Summary Asimov will provide technologies for cell line development and process development to researchers at the Goodman Cancer Institute. The company is developing a synthetic biology platform—from cells to software—to design and manufacture next-generation therapeutics, including biologics, cell and gene therapies, and RNA through a combination of products, services, and collaborations. Founded by bioengineers from MIT and Boston University and headquartered in Boston, the company has raised over $200 million from top institutional investors including Andreessen Horowitz, CPP Investments, Horizons Ventures, and Fidelity Management & Research Company. Asimov Announces Strategic Partnership with the Rosalind & Morris Goodman Cancer Institute at McGill University to Advance Next-Generation Biologics Asimov will provide cell line development and process development services to support promising biotherapeutic molecules identified through McGill’s oncology research programs June 18, 2026 07:00 ET | Source: Asimov Follow Asimov Share BOSTON, June  18, 2026  (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Asimov, the company building an AI-native synthetic biology platform to advance therapeutic development, today announced a strategic partnership with the Rosalind & Morris Goodman Cancer Institute (GCI) at McGill University. Through the collaboration, Asimov will provide cell line development and process development services to researchers at the GCI and McGill, supporting next-generation biologic candidates as they progress through IND-enabling studies and toward clinical development. “The GCI has a long track record of fundamental advances in cancer biology. As a strategic platform partner to the GCI, Asimov is excited to support the PIs and broader team as they continue pushing the boundaries of cancer research,” said Alec Nielsen, co-founder and CEO at Asimov. “As we expand our platform into earlier stages of drug discovery, including AI-powered multispecifics design, our goal is to make sure leading research institutions like the GCI have the best tools behind their science at every step.” “The GCI's mission is to translate fundamental cancer discovery into therapies that improve outcomes for patients, and that means giving our scientists access to technologies that match the molecular complexity of today's most promising therapeutic candidates,” said John Stagg, PhD, Director of the Rosalind and Morris Goodman Cancer Institute at McGill University. “Asimov's platform gives our researchers the ability to go from a promising design to a manufacturable molecule and a high-titer cell line, and we look forward to working with the Asimov team to shorten the distance between a discovery in our labs and a therapy that helps patients.” Over the past fifteen years, advances in clinical oncology have enabled the development of checkpoint inhibitors and other advanced biologics that have changed how clinicians treat cancer. The field is now moving rapidly into complex, multispecific architectures that engage multiple immune and tumor targets at once, opening up mechanisms that a single binder cannot achieve. Bridging the gap between discovery and manufacturability is still a major challenge for many researchers, as the bioproduction systems most academic labs have access to today were not designed for the level of molecular complexity seen in next-gen therapeutic candidates. As a result, many promising biologics are produced in relatively low-titer cell lines, creating a bottleneck on the path from a compelling discovery to a candidate ready for IND-enabling studies. The GCI sits at the forefront of efforts to translate fundamental cancer research into clinical outcomes, with research programs that integrate AI, computational biology, and advanced cancer modeling to uncover the mechanisms driving cancer and identify new therapeutic targets. Through this partnership, GCI and McGill researchers will gain access to Asimov's CHO Edge System, an AI-powered cell line development platform that routinely achieves titers of 8 to 12 g/L across biologic modalities, from standard IgGs to more complex architectures including multispecifics and fusion proteins. CHO Edge combines Asimov's proprietary GS knock-out CHO host, hyperactive transposase, and genetic parts library with Kernel, Asimov's computer-aided genetic design software, which uses AI models trained on Asimov's experimental database to recommend molecule-specific vector designs. BOSTON, June  18, 2026  (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Asimov, the company building an AI-native synthetic biology platform to advance therapeutic development, today announced a strategic partnership with the Rosalind & Morris Goodman Cancer Institute (GCI) at McGill University. Through the collaboration, Asimov will provide cell line development and process development services to researchers at the GCI and McGill, supporting next-generation biologic candidates as they progress through IND-enabling studies and toward clinical development.
“The GCI has a long track record of fundamental advances in cancer biology. As a strategic platform partner to the GCI, Asimov is excited to support the PIs and broader team as they continue pushing the boundaries of cancer research,” said Alec Nielsen, co-founder and CEO at Asimov. “As we expand our platform into earlier stages of drug discovery, including AI-powered multispecifics design, our goal is to make sure leading research institutions like the GCI have the best tools behind their science at every step.”
“The GCI's mission is to translate fundamental cancer discovery into therapies that improve outcomes for patients, and that means giving our scientists access to technologies that match the molecular complexity of today's most promising therapeutic candidates,” said John Stagg, PhD, Director of the Rosalind and Morris Goodman Cancer Institute at McGill University. “Asimov's platform gives our researchers the ability to go from a promising design to a manufacturable molecule and a high-titer cell line, and we look forward to working with the Asimov team to shorten the distance between a discovery in our labs and a therapy that helps patients.”
Over the past fifteen years, advances in clinical oncology have enabled the development of checkpoint inhibitors and other advanced biologics that have changed how clinicians treat cancer. The field is now moving rapidly into complex, multispecific architectures that engage multiple immune and tumor targets at once, opening up mechanisms that a single binder cannot achieve.
Bridging the gap between discovery and manufacturability is still a major challenge for many researchers, as the bioproduction systems most academic labs have access to today were not designed for the level of molecular complexity seen in next-gen therapeutic candidates. As a result, many promising biologics are produced in relatively low-titer cell lines, creating a bottleneck on the path from a compelling discovery to a candidate ready for IND-enabling studies. The GCI sits at the forefront of efforts to translate fundamental cancer research into clinical outcomes, with research programs that integrate AI, computational biology, and advanced cancer modeling to uncover the mechanisms driving cancer and identify new therapeutic targets.
Through this partnership, GCI and McGill researchers will gain access to Asimov's CHO Edge System, an AI-powered cell line development platform that routinely achieves titers of 8 to 12 g/L across biologic modalities, from standard IgGs to more complex architectures including multispecifics and fusion proteins. CHO Edge combines Asimov's proprietary GS knock-out CHO host, hyperactive transposase, and genetic parts library with Kernel, Asimov's computer-aided genetic design software, which uses AI models trained on Asimov's experimental database to recommend molecule-specific vector designs.

Asimov’s mission is to advance humanity’s ability to design living systems, enabling biotechnologies with outsized societal benefit. Skip to content Suggested Topics: Home Top Pharma Strategy Roche, a company built on oncology and diagnostics reinventing its pipeline Photo credits: Franck V Newsletter Signup - Under Article / In Page " * " indicates required fields There was a time when the numbers told an unoptimistic story about Roche. Its three oncology blockbusters, Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, had built it into the world’s largest cancer drug maker, collectively generating around $21 billion in annual sales at their peak. Then, biosimilars arrived. Deutsche Bank warned that Roche had $22 billion in revenues exposed to competition. In 2018, JP Morgan analysts forecast double-digit declines for its biggest products. When the erosion hit, it came harder and faster than Roche’s projections had anticipated; the $9.6 billion gap the company had modeled between 2018 and 2023 sales was crossed by 2021. But Roche grew through it, as a new generation of medicines – Ocrevus in multiple sclerosis, Hemlibra in hemophilia, and Vabysmo in retinal disease – absorbed the hit. By 2025, its top five growth drivers were pulling in over $20 billion in combined sales. But what has Roche chosen to do next? Beyond consolidating around its existing strengths, the company has launched a second strategic reinvention: entering obesity, placing a $7.1 billion bet on a novel inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) mechanism, and acquiring an allogeneic cell therapy platform, all within roughly two years. Table of contents What the Roche we know today was built on The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion The new blockbusters that filled the gap Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche How is Roche strategically positioned? A second reinvention for Roche What the Roche we know today was built on Back to Top The company that exists today is the product of two decisions made decades apart. In 1990, Roche acquired a 60% stake in the California biotech Genentech. The full buyout came in 2009, when Roche paid $46.8 billion for the remaining publicly held shares, at the time the largest acquisition in Swiss corporate history. The acquisition wasn’t as straightforward as it may seem. When Roche moved to buy out Genentech’s minority shareholders in July 2008, the initial offer of $89 per share triggered an immediate shareholder backlash. A class action lawsuit was filed alleging breach of fiduciary duty, with plaintiffs arguing Roche had deliberately timed the bid to exploit market turmoil and a weakened dollar. The litigation was eventually settled, but not before Roche was forced to raise its bid to $95 per share. For that price, Roche got more than just a drug portfolio; it got the platform that produced Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, the oncology trio that would come to define the company’s commercial identity for the next two decades. Herceptin changed HER2-positive breast cancer treatment. Avastin became a standard of care across multiple tumor types. Rituxan dominated blood cancer and later moved into autoimmune disease. They established Roche as the world’s largest oncology company, a position it held for years on the back of medicines that came almost entirely from Genentech’s labs. The other defining decision came earlier, in 1997, when Roche acquired Boehringer Mannheim for $11 billion. It gave Roche a diagnostics business to sit alongside its pharma business, at a time when most of its competitors were treating diagnostics as a secondary concern. The logic was that if you can develop the targeted therapy and the diagnostic test that identifies which patients will respond to it, you control both ends of the treatment decision. This became the foundation of what Roche calls personalized healthcare, a concept the company has used to frame its strategy for decades. The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion Back to Top In September 2019, Bill Anderson, CEO of Roche Pharmaceutical Division at the time, was estimating 60 to 70% erosion across Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, which he called a “conservative” projection. The company was looking at a CHF 9.6 billion ($11.8 billion) sales gap between 2018 and 2023. The dynamic was different from a conventional small-molecule patent cliff. Biosimilars to biologics face a slower and more complex uptake due to physician inertia, switching costs, and payer dynamics, all of which slow the erosion compared to generic drugs. Still, by Q1 2022, Avastin had shed 32% of its sales year-on-year, Herceptin 19%, and Rituxan 21%. On one side, Roche operated a deliberate pipeline rationalization: in early 2024, the company cut eight early- to mid-stage candidates. Five from neurology and three in oncology and hematology were discontinued as part of what CEO Thomas Schinecker described as a reallocation of resources toward programs with the clearest differentiation. On the other side, Roche made partnerships and acquisitions of de-risked external assets, with Schinecker stating that Roche was looking to acquire drug assets at all stages of development. The new blockbusters that filled the gap Back to Top In 2025, Roche’s new five main growth drivers, Ocrevus, Hemlibra, Vabysmo, Phesgo, and Xolair, generated combined sales of CHF 21.4 billion ($26.4 billion), up CHF 3.2 billion ($3.9 billion) from 2024. Ocrevus, an anti-CD20 antibody for multiple sclerosis approved in 2017, is Roche’s biggest seller at CHF 7.01 billion ($8.6 billion) in 2025. Hemlibra, a bispecific antibody for hemophilia A, reached CHF 4.8 billion ($5.9 billion), up 11% year-on-year. Vabysmo, a dual-targeting antibody for retinal diseases, launched in early 2022 and became one of Roche’s fastest-growing products. Suggested Articles How Daiichi Sankyo aims to become a top five oncology player by 2035 Blockbuster sales, lawsuits, and approvals: is there a recovery in sight for Bayer in 2026? Inside CSL’s strategy to simplify a biotech giant The company made cautious bets in areas where it had expertise. Ocrevus extended Roche’s existing antibody expertise into a new indication. Hemlibra and Vabysmo both used the bispecific antibody format, a platform Roche had been developing internally for years. Phesgo was a lifecycle management of the HER2 franchise. Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche Back to Top Since 2023, Roche has deployed significant capital towards dealmaking. At the JPMorgan Healthcare Conference in January 2025, Roche Pharmaceutical Division’s CEO Teresa Graham said Roche has roughly $10 billion in M&A firepower it can deploy each year, and is ready to use it “if and when we find transformative assets that are either complementary to our portfolio or change the game in important diseases.” Across different therapeutic areas, two types dominate in Roche’s dealmaking strategy. The first is large-scale acquisitions of late-stage or phase-3-ready assets where the clinical risk is substantially de-risked. First, Telavant in October 2023 for $7.1 billion upfront , plus a $150 million near-term milestone, giving Roche U.S. and Japan rights to afimkibart, an anti-TL1A antibody with strong phase 2b data in ulcerative colitis and a novel mechanism targeting both inflammation and fibrosis. Six weeks later, Carmot Therapeutics for $2.7 billion upfront plus up to $400 million in milestones, bringing three clinical-stage obesity candidates, including CT-388, a phase-2-ready dual GLP-1/GIP agonist. Then, 89bio in September 2025, $2.4 billion upfront and up to $3.5 billion total, for pegozafermin, an FGF21 analog in phase 3 for MASH. The second deal type is smaller and more targeted. Poseida Therapeutics in November 2024 for up to $1.5 billion, bringing an allogeneic CAR-T platform built on non-viral, T stem cell memory-rich engineering, a technology Roche had already been partnering with since 2022. Regor Therapeutics in September 2024 for $850 million upfront, technically an asset purchase through Genentech, bringing two next-generation CDK inhibitors for breast cancer, positioned as potential successors to the CDK4/6 inhibitor class. And in March 2025, the Zealand Pharma collaboration : $1.65 billion upfront against a total potential value of $5.3 billion to co-develop petrelintide, an amylin analog being positioned as a combination partner for CT-388 in obesity. These deals represent commitments of well over $15 billion in upfront capital across roughly two years, in IBD, obesity, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), cell therapy, and breast cancer oncology. How is Roche strategically positioned? Back to Top The most ambitious expansion Roche has operated in the past few years might be towards obesity. But it has also invested significantly in immunology, as well as neuroscience. Roche’s obesity pipeline: the most audacious bet Roche’s obesity pipeline strategy looks like a portfolio bet on where the market will be in five to seven years. The lead asset is CT-388, a once-weekly injectable dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist, the same mechanistic class as Eli Lilly’s Zepbound. In January 2026, Roche published phase 2 data showing placebo-adjusted weight loss of 22.5% at 48 weeks at the highest dose tested, without reaching a plateau. Two phase 3 trials are now underway, initiated in Q1 2026. The timing might not be the most convenient, as CT-388 is entering phase 3 at a point when Wegovy and Zepbound already have years of commercial infrastructure, physician familiarity, and patient uptake behind them. Even if the phase 3 trials deliver, Roche is behind its competition. Roche’s answer to that timing problem is the combination strategy. The obesity market today is dominated by single-agent GLP-1s, but their key limitation is tolerability, with nausea driving most discontinuation, and concerns about muscle mass loss on long-term treatment. Roche is betting that by the time it arrives, combinations addressing those limitations will be the new standard. Petrelintide, acquired via the $1.65 billion upfront Zealand Pharma collaboration, is an amylin analog designed to complement GLP-1 therapy by reducing nausea and preserving lean mass, with a combination study with CT-388 planned for the first half of 2026. However, not all bets can go all the way, and in March 2026, Roche announced it would discontinue Emugrobart, an anti-myostatin antibody meant to address lean mass preservation that was tested in combination with Mounjaro. Bowel disease and immunology: inside Roche’s $7 billion bet on TL1A IBD is a large and commercially active market, but it remains one where current therapies fall short of what patients need. Despite a generation of biologics, TNF inhibitors, IL-12/23 antibodies, and integrin blockers, most patients with ulcerative colitis or Crohn’s disease do not achieve lasting remission. This is what made TL1A, a cytokine involved in both inflammation and fibrosis, one of the most closely watched targets in immunology over the past few years. TL1A is distinct from most existing IBD targets because it drives immune activation in addition to fibrosis, the scarring that causes long-term complications in Crohn’s disease. An antibody that addresses both processes could do more than current biologics. That is the bet behind afimkibart, the anti-TL1A antibody Roche acquired with Telavant. Phase 3 trials in ulcerative colitis are ongoing, with a regulatory filing expected in 2027, and a phase 2 in Crohn’s disease also underway. However, the TL1A space has become one of the most crowded in immunology: Merck, Sanofi/Teva, AbbVie, Spyre Therapeutics, and Boehringer Ingelheim are all pursuing the target. And in June 2026, Merck announced that its TL1A antibody tulisokibart had delivered the first positive phase 3 results in ulcerative colitis, beating Roche to the clinical milestone. Roche’s afimkibart had been seen as a frontrunner, but Merck has now moved ahead. Roche’s filings are expected in 2027, which means it will likely be a second or third entrant, potentially a different commercial proposition than the $7.1 billion price tag, and making differentiation more important than before. Cell therapy and neuroscience: Roche’s new bets and unfinished business The Poseida acquisition gives Roche something it didn’t have: a credible allogeneic CAR-T platform. Current autologous CAR-T therapies require manufacturing a product from each patient’s own cells, which is time-consuming, expensive, and logistically complex. Allogeneic therapies use donor cells manufactured in advance, enabling faster access and potentially lower costs. Poseida’s lead candidate, P-BCMA-ALLO1, targeting multiple myeloma, showed a 91% overall response rate in phase 1 data in heavily pretreated patients. The platform also has applications in autoimmune disease, an area of growing interest. On the neuroscience side, Roche has a painful history in neurodegeneration with gantenerumab failing in phase 3 Alzheimer’s trials in 2022, following earlier setbacks with crenezumab and other pipeline candidates. It is in this context that Roche is advancing trontinemab, its next Alzheimer’s candidate. Trontinemab is different from gantenerumab and uses Roche’s proprietary Brainshuttle technology to carry the antibody across the blood-brain barrier via the transferrin receptor, achieving faster and deeper amyloid clearance than prior antibodies. Phase 2 data showed 91% of participants became amyloid PET-negative after six months. Two phase 3 trials have been initiated, but the question is also whether amyloid clearance will translate to cognitive benefit, which is the question that has challenged Alzheimer’s programs before. Then there’s Roche’s ambiguous Parkinson’s story with Prasinezumab for Parkinson’s. The phase 2 Padova trial missed its primary endpoint, but Roche has committed to phase 3, citing that the totality of data suggests the drug could be the first disease-modifying candidate. That is a defensible call given the near-total absence of disease-modifying options in Parkinson’s , but it also requires the phase 3 to deliver where the phase 2 could not. A second reinvention for Roche Back to Top Roche navigated the biosimilar cliff by staying close to what it knew: bispecific antibodies, lifecycle management, and next-generation iterations in established biology. The strategy now feels different as the company is stepping out of what it was built on. Obesity, IBD, allogeneic cell therapy, and neurodegeneration are not extensions of an existing franchise. They are new territory and often in markets that are already competitive and, in some cases, already moving faster than Roche anticipated. The company is well established and has the financial discipline, the infrastructure, and the dealmaking capabilities to compete. But this isn’t proof that the combination thesis in obesity will outpace Lilly and Novo Nordisk’s head start, that afimkibart can differentiate in a TL1A field where Merck has now moved first, or that trontinemab will become the Alzheimer’s success Roche has been looking for. Roche now looks like a company built on oncology and diagnostics, reinventing itself across several therapeutic areas at once. Explore other topics: Obesity Roche ADVERTISEMENT Latest Articles “It’s time to Act”: EU biotech to undergo major changes De-risking neurology drug development with better mouse models Bronchiectasis treatment after brensocatib: where the field goes next 8 of the most expensive drugs in biopharma: Unraveling the costliest medications Subscribe to our newsletter Get the latest biotech news directly to your inbox! However, the TL1A space has become one of the most crowded in immunology: Merck, Sanofi/Teva, AbbVie, Spyre Therapeutics, and Boehringer Ingelheim are all pursuing the target. And in June 2026, Merck announced that its TL1A antibody tulisokibart had delivered the first positive phase 3 results in ulcerative colitis, beating Roche to the clinical milestone. Roche’s afimkibart had been seen as a frontrunner, but Merck has now moved ahead. Roche’s filings are expected in 2027, which means it will likely be a second or third entrant, potentially a different commercial proposition than the $7.1 billion price tag, and making differentiation more important than before. Cell therapy and neuroscience: Roche’s new bets and unfinished business The Poseida acquisition gives Roche something it didn’t have: a credible allogeneic CAR-T platform. Current autologous CAR-T therapies require manufacturing a product from each patient’s own cells, which is time-consuming, expensive, and logistically complex. Allogeneic therapies use donor cells manufactured in advance, enabling faster access and potentially lower costs. Poseida’s lead candidate, P-BCMA-ALLO1, targeting multiple myeloma, showed a 91% overall response rate in phase 1 data in heavily pretreated patients. The platform also has applications in autoimmune disease, an area of growing interest. On the neuroscience side, Roche has a painful history in neurodegeneration with gantenerumab failing in phase 3 Alzheimer’s trials in 2022, following earlier setbacks with crenezumab and other pipeline candidates. It is in this context that Roche is advancing trontinemab, its next Alzheimer’s candidate. Trontinemab is different from gantenerumab and uses Roche’s proprietary Brainshuttle technology to carry the antibody across the blood-brain barrier via the transferrin receptor, achieving faster and deeper amyloid clearance than prior antibodies. Phase 2 data showed 91% of participants became amyloid PET-negative after six months. Two phase 3 trials have been initiated, but the question is also whether amyloid clearance will translate to cognitive benefit, which is the question that has challenged Alzheimer’s programs before. Then there’s Roche’s ambiguous Parkinson’s story with Prasinezumab for Parkinson’s. The phase 2 Padova trial missed its primary endpoint, but Roche has committed to phase 3, citing that the totality of data suggests the drug could be the first disease-modifying candidate. That is a defensible call given the near-total absence of disease-modifying options in Parkinson’s , but it also requires the phase 3 to deliver where the phase 2 could not. A second reinvention for Roche Back to Top Roche navigated the biosimilar cliff by staying close to what it knew: bispecific antibodies, lifecycle management, and next-generation iterations in established biology. The strategy now feels different as the company is stepping out of what it was built on. Obesity, IBD, allogeneic cell therapy, and neurodegeneration are not extensions of an existing franchise. They are new territory and often in markets that are already competitive and, in some cases, already moving faster than Roche anticipated. The company is well established and has the financial discipline, the infrastructure, and the dealmaking capabilities to compete. But this isn’t proof that the combination thesis in obesity will outpace Lilly and Novo Nordisk’s head start, that afimkibart can differentiate in a TL1A field where Merck has now moved first, or that trontinemab will become the Alzheimer’s success Roche has been looking for. Roche now looks like a company built on oncology and diagnostics, reinventing itself across several therapeutic areas at once. Top Pharma Strategy
Roche, a company built on oncology and diagnostics reinventing its pipeline
Photo credits: Franck V Newsletter Signup - Under Article / In Page " * " indicates required fields There was a time when the numbers told an unoptimistic story about Roche. Its three oncology blockbusters, Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, had built it into the world’s largest cancer drug maker, collectively generating around $21 billion in annual sales at their peak. Then, biosimilars arrived. Deutsche Bank warned that Roche had $22 billion in revenues exposed to competition. In 2018, JP Morgan analysts forecast double-digit declines for its biggest products. When the erosion hit, it came harder and faster than Roche’s projections had anticipated; the $9.6 billion gap the company had modeled between 2018 and 2023 sales was crossed by 2021. But Roche grew through it, as a new generation of medicines – Ocrevus in multiple sclerosis, Hemlibra in hemophilia, and Vabysmo in retinal disease – absorbed the hit. By 2025, its top five growth drivers were pulling in over $20 billion in combined sales. But what has Roche chosen to do next? Beyond consolidating around its existing strengths, the company has launched a second strategic reinvention: entering obesity, placing a $7.1 billion bet on a novel inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) mechanism, and acquiring an allogeneic cell therapy platform, all within roughly two years. Table of contents What the Roche we know today was built on The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion The new blockbusters that filled the gap Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche How is Roche strategically positioned? A second reinvention for Roche What the Roche we know today was built on Back to Top The company that exists today is the product of two decisions made decades apart. In 1990, Roche acquired a 60% stake in the California biotech Genentech. The full buyout came in 2009, when Roche paid $46.8 billion for the remaining publicly held shares, at the time the largest acquisition in Swiss corporate history. The acquisition wasn’t as straightforward as it may seem. When Roche moved to buy out Genentech’s minority shareholders in July 2008, the initial offer of $89 per share triggered an immediate shareholder backlash. A class action lawsuit was filed alleging breach of fiduciary duty, with plaintiffs arguing Roche had deliberately timed the bid to exploit market turmoil and a weakened dollar. The litigation was eventually settled, but not before Roche was forced to raise its bid to $95 per share. For that price, Roche got more than just a drug portfolio; it got the platform that produced Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, the oncology trio that would come to define the company’s commercial identity for the next two decades. Herceptin changed HER2-positive breast cancer treatment. Avastin became a standard of care across multiple tumor types. Rituxan dominated blood cancer and later moved into autoimmune disease. They established Roche as the world’s largest oncology company, a position it held for years on the back of medicines that came almost entirely from Genentech’s labs. The other defining decision came earlier, in 1997, when Roche acquired Boehringer Mannheim for $11 billion. It gave Roche a diagnostics business to sit alongside its pharma business, at a time when most of its competitors were treating diagnostics as a secondary concern. The logic was that if you can develop the targeted therapy and the diagnostic test that identifies which patients will respond to it, you control both ends of the treatment decision. This became the foundation of what Roche calls personalized healthcare, a concept the company has used to frame its strategy for decades. The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion Back to Top In September 2019, Bill Anderson, CEO of Roche Pharmaceutical Division at the time, was estimating 60 to 70% erosion across Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, which he called a “conservative” projection. The company was looking at a CHF 9.6 billion ($11.8 billion) sales gap between 2018 and 2023. The dynamic was different from a conventional small-molecule patent cliff. Biosimilars to biologics face a slower and more complex uptake due to physician inertia, switching costs, and payer dynamics, all of which slow the erosion compared to generic drugs. Still, by Q1 2022, Avastin had shed 32% of its sales year-on-year, Herceptin 19%, and Rituxan 21%. On one side, Roche operated a deliberate pipeline rationalization: in early 2024, the company cut eight early- to mid-stage candidates. Five from neurology and three in oncology and hematology were discontinued as part of what CEO Thomas Schinecker described as a reallocation of resources toward programs with the clearest differentiation. On the other side, Roche made partnerships and acquisitions of de-risked external assets, with Schinecker stating that Roche was looking to acquire drug assets at all stages of development. The new blockbusters that filled the gap Back to Top In 2025, Roche’s new five main growth drivers, Ocrevus, Hemlibra, Vabysmo, Phesgo, and Xolair, generated combined sales of CHF 21.4 billion ($26.4 billion), up CHF 3.2 billion ($3.9 billion) from 2024. Ocrevus, an anti-CD20 antibody for multiple sclerosis approved in 2017, is Roche’s biggest seller at CHF 7.01 billion ($8.6 billion) in 2025. Hemlibra, a bispecific antibody for hemophilia A, reached CHF 4.8 billion ($5.9 billion), up 11% year-on-year. Vabysmo, a dual-targeting antibody for retinal diseases, launched in early 2022 and became one of Roche’s fastest-growing products. Suggested Articles How Daiichi Sankyo aims to become a top five oncology player by 2035 Blockbuster sales, lawsuits, and approvals: is there a recovery in sight for Bayer in 2026? Inside CSL’s strategy to simplify a biotech giant The company made cautious bets in areas where it had expertise. Ocrevus extended Roche’s existing antibody expertise into a new indication. Hemlibra and Vabysmo both used the bispecific antibody format, a platform Roche had been developing internally for years. Phesgo was a lifecycle management of the HER2 franchise. Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche Back to Top Since 2023, Roche has deployed significant capital towards dealmaking. At the JPMorgan Healthcare Conference in January 2025, Roche Pharmaceutical Division’s CEO Teresa Graham said Roche has roughly $10 billion in M&A firepower it can deploy each year, and is ready to use it “if and when we find transformative assets that are either complementary to our portfolio or change the game in important diseases.” Across different therapeutic areas, two types dominate in Roche’s dealmaking strategy. The first is large-scale acquisitions of late-stage or phase-3-ready assets where the clinical risk is substantially de-risked. First, Telavant in October 2023 for $7.1 billion upfront , plus a $150 million near-term milestone, giving Roche U.S. and Japan rights to afimkibart, an anti-TL1A antibody with strong phase 2b data in ulcerative colitis and a novel mechanism targeting both inflammation and fibrosis. Six weeks later, Carmot Therapeutics for $2.7 billion upfront plus up to $400 million in milestones, bringing three clinical-stage obesity candidates, including CT-388, a phase-2-ready dual GLP-1/GIP agonist. Then, 89bio in September 2025, $2.4 billion upfront and up to $3.5 billion total, for pegozafermin, an FGF21 analog in phase 3 for MASH. The second deal type is smaller and more targeted. Poseida Therapeutics in November 2024 for up to $1.5 billion, bringing an allogeneic CAR-T platform built on non-viral, T stem cell memory-rich engineering, a technology Roche had already been partnering with since 2022. Regor Therapeutics in September 2024 for $850 million upfront, technically an asset purchase through Genentech, bringing two next-generation CDK inhibitors for breast cancer, positioned as potential successors to the CDK4/6 inhibitor class. And in March 2025, the Zealand Pharma collaboration : $1.65 billion upfront against a total potential value of $5.3 billion to co-develop petrelintide, an amylin analog being positioned as a combination partner for CT-388 in obesity. These deals represent commitments of well over $15 billion in upfront capital across roughly two years, in IBD, obesity, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), cell therapy, and breast cancer oncology. How is Roche strategically positioned? Back to Top The most ambitious expansion Roche has operated in the past few years might be towards obesity. But it has also invested significantly in immunology, as well as neuroscience. Roche’s obesity pipeline: the most audacious bet Roche’s obesity pipeline strategy looks like a portfolio bet on where the market will be in five to seven years. The lead asset is CT-388, a once-weekly injectable dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist, the same mechanistic class as Eli Lilly’s Zepbound. In January 2026, Roche published phase 2 data showing placebo-adjusted weight loss of 22.5% at 48 weeks at the highest dose tested, without reaching a plateau. Two phase 3 trials are now underway, initiated in Q1 2026. The timing might not be the most convenient, as CT-388 is entering phase 3 at a point when Wegovy and Zepbound already have years of commercial infrastructure, physician familiarity, and patient uptake behind them. Even if the phase 3 trials deliver, Roche is behind its competition. Roche’s answer to that timing problem is the combination strategy. The obesity market today is dominated by single-agent GLP-1s, but their key limitation is tolerability, with nausea driving most discontinuation, and concerns about muscle mass loss on long-term treatment. Roche is betting that by the time it arrives, combinations addressing those limitations will be the new standard. Petrelintide, acquired via the $1.65 billion upfront Zealand Pharma collaboration, is an amylin analog designed to complement GLP-1 therapy by reducing nausea and preserving lean mass, with a combination study with CT-388 planned for the first half of 2026. However, not all bets can go all the way, and in March 2026, Roche announced it would discontinue Emugrobart, an anti-myostatin antibody meant to address lean mass preservation that was tested in combination with Mounjaro. Bowel disease and immunology: inside Roche’s $7 billion bet on TL1A IBD is a large and commercially active market, but it remains one where current therapies fall short of what patients need. Despite a generation of biologics, TNF inhibitors, IL-12/23 antibodies, and integrin blockers, most patients with ulcerative colitis or Crohn’s disease do not achieve lasting remission. This is what made TL1A, a cytokine involved in both inflammation and fibrosis, one of the most closely watched targets in immunology over the past few years. TL1A is distinct from most existing IBD targets because it drives immune activation in addition to fibrosis, the scarring that causes long-term complications in Crohn’s disease. An antibody that addresses both processes could do more than current biologics. That is the bet behind afimkibart, the anti-TL1A antibody Roche acquired with Telavant. Phase 3 trials in ulcerative colitis are ongoing, with a regulatory filing expected in 2027, and a phase 2 in Crohn’s disease also underway. However, the TL1A space has become one of the most crowded in immunology: Merck, Sanofi/Teva, AbbVie, Spyre Therapeutics, and Boehringer Ingelheim are all pursuing the target. And in June 2026, Merck announced that its TL1A antibody tulisokibart had delivered the first positive phase 3 results in ulcerative colitis, beating Roche to the clinical milestone. Roche’s afimkibart had been seen as a frontrunner, but Merck has now moved ahead. Roche’s filings are expected in 2027, which means it will likely be a second or third entrant, potentially a different commercial proposition than the $7.1 billion price tag, and making differentiation more important than before. Cell therapy and neuroscience: Roche’s new bets and unfinished business The Poseida acquisition gives Roche something it didn’t have: a credible allogeneic CAR-T platform. Current autologous CAR-T therapies require manufacturing a product from each patient’s own cells, which is time-consuming, expensive, and logistically complex. Allogeneic therapies use donor cells manufactured in advance, enabling faster access and potentially lower costs. Poseida’s lead candidate, P-BCMA-ALLO1, targeting multiple myeloma, showed a 91% overall response rate in phase 1 data in heavily pretreated patients. The platform also has applications in autoimmune disease, an area of growing interest. On the neuroscience side, Roche has a painful history in neurodegeneration with gantenerumab failing in phase 3 Alzheimer’s trials in 2022, following earlier setbacks with crenezumab and other pipeline candidates. It is in this context that Roche is advancing trontinemab, its next Alzheimer’s candidate. Trontinemab is different from gantenerumab and uses Roche’s proprietary Brainshuttle technology to carry the antibody across the blood-brain barrier via the transferrin receptor, achieving faster and deeper amyloid clearance than prior antibodies. Phase 2 data showed 91% of participants became amyloid PET-negative after six months. Two phase 3 trials have been initiated, but the question is also whether amyloid clearance will translate to cognitive benefit, which is the question that has challenged Alzheimer’s programs before. Then there’s Roche’s ambiguous Parkinson’s story with Prasinezumab for Parkinson’s. The phase 2 Padova trial missed its primary endpoint, but Roche has committed to phase 3, citing that the totality of data suggests the drug could be the first disease-modifying candidate. That is a defensible call given the near-total absence of disease-modifying options in Parkinson’s , but it also requires the phase 3 to deliver where the phase 2 could not. A second reinvention for Roche Back to Top Roche navigated the biosimilar cliff by staying close to what it knew: bispecific antibodies, lifecycle management, and next-generation iterations in established biology. The strategy now feels different as the company is stepping out of what it was built on. Obesity, IBD, allogeneic cell therapy, and neurodegeneration are not extensions of an existing franchise. They are new territory and often in markets that are already competitive and, in some cases, already moving faster than Roche anticipated. The company is well established and has the financial discipline, the infrastructure, and the dealmaking capabilities to compete. But this isn’t proof that the combination thesis in obesity will outpace Lilly and Novo Nordisk’s head start, that afimkibart can differentiate in a TL1A field where Merck has now moved first, or that trontinemab will become the Alzheimer’s success Roche has been looking for. The strategy now feels different as the company is stepping out of what it was built on. Obesity, IBD, allogeneic cell therapy, and neurodegeneration are not extensions of an existing franchise. They are new territory and often in markets that are already competitive and, in some cases, already moving faster than Roche anticipated. The company is well established and has the financial discipline, the infrastructure, and the dealmaking capabilities to compete. But this isn’t proof that the combination thesis in obesity will outpace Lilly and Novo Nordisk’s head start, that afimkibart can differentiate in a TL1A field where Merck has now moved first, or that trontinemab will become the Alzheimer’s success Roche has been looking for. Roche now looks like a company built on oncology and diagnostics, reinventing itself across several therapeutic areas at once. Explore other topics: Obesity Roche ADVERTISEMENT
Newsletter Signup - Under Article / In Page " * " indicates required fields There was a time when the numbers told an unoptimistic story about Roche. Its three oncology blockbusters, Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, had built it into the world’s largest cancer drug maker, collectively generating around $21 billion in annual sales at their peak. Then, biosimilars arrived. Deutsche Bank warned that Roche had $22 billion in revenues exposed to competition. In 2018, JP Morgan analysts forecast double-digit declines for its biggest products. When the erosion hit, it came harder and faster than Roche’s projections had anticipated; the $9.6 billion gap the company had modeled between 2018 and 2023 sales was crossed by 2021. But Roche grew through it, as a new generation of medicines – Ocrevus in multiple sclerosis, Hemlibra in hemophilia, and Vabysmo in retinal disease – absorbed the hit. By 2025, its top five growth drivers were pulling in over $20 billion in combined sales. But what has Roche chosen to do next? Beyond consolidating around its existing strengths, the company has launched a second strategic reinvention: entering obesity, placing a $7.1 billion bet on a novel inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) mechanism, and acquiring an allogeneic cell therapy platform, all within roughly two years. Table of contents What the Roche we know today was built on The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion The new blockbusters that filled the gap Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche How is Roche strategically positioned? A second reinvention for Roche What the Roche we know today was built on Back to Top The company that exists today is the product of two decisions made decades apart. In 1990, Roche acquired a 60% stake in the California biotech Genentech. The full buyout came in 2009, when Roche paid $46.8 billion for the remaining publicly held shares, at the time the largest acquisition in Swiss corporate history. The acquisition wasn’t as straightforward as it may seem. When Roche moved to buy out Genentech’s minority shareholders in July 2008, the initial offer of $89 per share triggered an immediate shareholder backlash. A class action lawsuit was filed alleging breach of fiduciary duty, with plaintiffs arguing Roche had deliberately timed the bid to exploit market turmoil and a weakened dollar. The litigation was eventually settled, but not before Roche was forced to raise its bid to $95 per share. For that price, Roche got more than just a drug portfolio; it got the platform that produced Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, the oncology trio that would come to define the company’s commercial identity for the next two decades. Herceptin changed HER2-positive breast cancer treatment. Avastin became a standard of care across multiple tumor types. Rituxan dominated blood cancer and later moved into autoimmune disease. They established Roche as the world’s largest oncology company, a position it held for years on the back of medicines that came almost entirely from Genentech’s labs. The other defining decision came earlier, in 1997, when Roche acquired Boehringer Mannheim for $11 billion. It gave Roche a diagnostics business to sit alongside its pharma business, at a time when most of its competitors were treating diagnostics as a secondary concern. The logic was that if you can develop the targeted therapy and the diagnostic test that identifies which patients will respond to it, you control both ends of the treatment decision. This became the foundation of what Roche calls personalized healthcare, a concept the company has used to frame its strategy for decades. The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion Back to Top In September 2019, Bill Anderson, CEO of Roche Pharmaceutical Division at the time, was estimating 60 to 70% erosion across Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, which he called a “conservative” projection. The company was looking at a CHF 9.6 billion ($11.8 billion) sales gap between 2018 and 2023. The dynamic was different from a conventional small-molecule patent cliff. Biosimilars to biologics face a slower and more complex uptake due to physician inertia, switching costs, and payer dynamics, all of which slow the erosion compared to generic drugs. Still, by Q1 2022, Avastin had shed 32% of its sales year-on-year, Herceptin 19%, and Rituxan 21%. On one side, Roche operated a deliberate pipeline rationalization: in early 2024, the company cut eight early- to mid-stage candidates. Five from neurology and three in oncology and hematology were discontinued as part of what CEO Thomas Schinecker described as a reallocation of resources toward programs with the clearest differentiation. On the other side, Roche made partnerships and acquisitions of de-risked external assets, with Schinecker stating that Roche was looking to acquire drug assets at all stages of development. The new blockbusters that filled the gap Back to Top In 2025, Roche’s new five main growth drivers, Ocrevus, Hemlibra, Vabysmo, Phesgo, and Xolair, generated combined sales of CHF 21.4 billion ($26.4 billion), up CHF 3.2 billion ($3.9 billion) from 2024. Ocrevus, an anti-CD20 antibody for multiple sclerosis approved in 2017, is Roche’s biggest seller at CHF 7.01 billion ($8.6 billion) in 2025. Hemlibra, a bispecific antibody for hemophilia A, reached CHF 4.8 billion ($5.9 billion), up 11% year-on-year. Vabysmo, a dual-targeting antibody for retinal diseases, launched in early 2022 and became one of Roche’s fastest-growing products. Suggested Articles How Daiichi Sankyo aims to become a top five oncology player by 2035 Blockbuster sales, lawsuits, and approvals: is there a recovery in sight for Bayer in 2026? Inside CSL’s strategy to simplify a biotech giant The company made cautious bets in areas where it had expertise. Ocrevus extended Roche’s existing antibody expertise into a new indication. Hemlibra and Vabysmo both used the bispecific antibody format, a platform Roche had been developing internally for years. Phesgo was a lifecycle management of the HER2 franchise. Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche Back to Top Since 2023, Roche has deployed significant capital towards dealmaking. At the JPMorgan Healthcare Conference in January 2025, Roche Pharmaceutical Division’s CEO Teresa Graham said Roche has roughly $10 billion in M&A firepower it can deploy each year, and is ready to use it “if and when we find transformative assets that are either complementary to our portfolio or change the game in important diseases.” Across different therapeutic areas, two types dominate in Roche’s dealmaking strategy. The first is large-scale acquisitions of late-stage or phase-3-ready assets where the clinical risk is substantially de-risked. First, Telavant in October 2023 for $7.1 billion upfront , plus a $150 million near-term milestone, giving Roche U.S. and Japan rights to afimkibart, an anti-TL1A antibody with strong phase 2b data in ulcerative colitis and a novel mechanism targeting both inflammation and fibrosis. Six weeks later, Carmot Therapeutics for $2.7 billion upfront plus up to $400 million in milestones, bringing three clinical-stage obesity candidates, including CT-388, a phase-2-ready dual GLP-1/GIP agonist. Then, 89bio in September 2025, $2.4 billion upfront and up to $3.5 billion total, for pegozafermin, an FGF21 analog in phase 3 for MASH. The second deal type is smaller and more targeted. Poseida Therapeutics in November 2024 for up to $1.5 billion, bringing an allogeneic CAR-T platform built on non-viral, T stem cell memory-rich engineering, a technology Roche had already been partnering with since 2022. Regor Therapeutics in September 2024 for $850 million upfront, technically an asset purchase through Genentech, bringing two next-generation CDK inhibitors for breast cancer, positioned as potential successors to the CDK4/6 inhibitor class. And in March 2025, the Zealand Pharma collaboration : $1.65 billion upfront against a total potential value of $5.3 billion to co-develop petrelintide, an amylin analog being positioned as a combination partner for CT-388 in obesity. These deals represent commitments of well over $15 billion in upfront capital across roughly two years, in IBD, obesity, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), cell therapy, and breast cancer oncology. How is Roche strategically positioned? Back to Top The most ambitious expansion Roche has operated in the past few years might be towards obesity. But it has also invested significantly in immunology, as well as neuroscience. Roche’s obesity pipeline: the most audacious bet Roche’s obesity pipeline strategy looks like a portfolio bet on where the market will be in five to seven years. The lead asset is CT-388, a once-weekly injectable dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist, the same mechanistic class as Eli Lilly’s Zepbound. In January 2026, Roche published phase 2 data showing placebo-adjusted weight loss of 22.5% at 48 weeks at the highest dose tested, without reaching a plateau. Two phase 3 trials are now underway, initiated in Q1 2026. The timing might not be the most convenient, as CT-388 is entering phase 3 at a point when Wegovy and Zepbound already have years of commercial infrastructure, physician familiarity, and patient uptake behind them. Even if the phase 3 trials deliver, Roche is behind its competition. Roche’s answer to that timing problem is the combination strategy. The obesity market today is dominated by single-agent GLP-1s, but their key limitation is tolerability, with nausea driving most discontinuation, and concerns about muscle mass loss on long-term treatment. Roche is betting that by the time it arrives, combinations addressing those limitations will be the new standard. Petrelintide, acquired via the $1.65 billion upfront Zealand Pharma collaboration, is an amylin analog designed to complement GLP-1 therapy by reducing nausea and preserving lean mass, with a combination study with CT-388 planned for the first half of 2026. However, not all bets can go all the way, and in March 2026, Roche announced it would discontinue Emugrobart, an anti-myostatin antibody meant to address lean mass preservation that was tested in combination with Mounjaro. Bowel disease and immunology: inside Roche’s $7 billion bet on TL1A IBD is a large and commercially active market, but it remains one where current therapies fall short of what patients need. Despite a generation of biologics, TNF inhibitors, IL-12/23 antibodies, and integrin blockers, most patients with ulcerative colitis or Crohn’s disease do not achieve lasting remission. This is what made TL1A, a cytokine involved in both inflammation and fibrosis, one of the most closely watched targets in immunology over the past few years. TL1A is distinct from most existing IBD targets because it drives immune activation in addition to fibrosis, the scarring that causes long-term complications in Crohn’s disease. An antibody that addresses both processes could do more than current biologics. That is the bet behind afimkibart, the anti-TL1A antibody Roche acquired with Telavant. Phase 3 trials in ulcerative colitis are ongoing, with a regulatory filing expected in 2027, and a phase 2 in Crohn’s disease also underway. However, the TL1A space has become one of the most crowded in immunology: Merck, Sanofi/Teva, AbbVie, Spyre Therapeutics, and Boehringer Ingelheim are all pursuing the target. And in June 2026, Merck announced that its TL1A antibody tulisokibart had delivered the first positive phase 3 results in ulcerative colitis, beating Roche to the clinical milestone. Roche’s afimkibart had been seen as a frontrunner, but Merck has now moved ahead. Roche’s filings are expected in 2027, which means it will likely be a second or third entrant, potentially a different commercial proposition than the $7.1 billion price tag, and making differentiation more important than before. Cell therapy and neuroscience: Roche’s new bets and unfinished business The Poseida acquisition gives Roche something it didn’t have: a credible allogeneic CAR-T platform. Current autologous CAR-T therapies require manufacturing a product from each patient’s own cells, which is time-consuming, expensive, and logistically complex. Allogeneic therapies use donor cells manufactured in advance, enabling faster access and potentially lower costs. Poseida’s lead candidate, P-BCMA-ALLO1, targeting multiple myeloma, showed a 91% overall response rate in phase 1 data in heavily pretreated patients. The platform also has applications in autoimmune disease, an area of growing interest. On the neuroscience side, Roche has a painful history in neurodegeneration with gantenerumab failing in phase 3 Alzheimer’s trials in 2022, following earlier setbacks with crenezumab and other pipeline candidates. It is in this context that Roche is advancing trontinemab, its next Alzheimer’s candidate. Trontinemab is different from gantenerumab and uses Roche’s proprietary Brainshuttle technology to carry the antibody across the blood-brain barrier via the transferrin receptor, achieving faster and deeper amyloid clearance than prior antibodies. Phase 2 data showed 91% of participants became amyloid PET-negative after six months. Two phase 3 trials have been initiated, but the question is also whether amyloid clearance will translate to cognitive benefit, which is the question that has challenged Alzheimer’s programs before. Then there’s Roche’s ambiguous Parkinson’s story with Prasinezumab for Parkinson’s. The phase 2 Padova trial missed its primary endpoint, but Roche has committed to phase 3, citing that the totality of data suggests the drug could be the first disease-modifying candidate. That is a defensible call given the near-total absence of disease-modifying options in Parkinson’s , but it also requires the phase 3 to deliver where the phase 2 could not. A second reinvention for Roche Back to Top Roche navigated the biosimilar cliff by staying close to what it knew: bispecific antibodies, lifecycle management, and next-generation iterations in established biology. The strategy now feels different as the company is stepping out of what it was built on. Obesity, IBD, allogeneic cell therapy, and neurodegeneration are not extensions of an existing franchise. They are new territory and often in markets that are already competitive and, in some cases, already moving faster than Roche anticipated. The company is well established and has the financial discipline, the infrastructure, and the dealmaking capabilities to compete. But this isn’t proof that the combination thesis in obesity will outpace Lilly and Novo Nordisk’s head start, that afimkibart can differentiate in a TL1A field where Merck has now moved first, or that trontinemab will become the Alzheimer’s success Roche has been looking for. Roche now looks like a company built on oncology and diagnostics, reinventing itself across several therapeutic areas at once. Explore other topics: Obesity Roche
